prompt.name: setup
prompt.content:
Attention: The following instructions must be followed in order to successfully set up Storybook in this project. Do not skip steps or attempt to do them out of order.

Your goal is to make Storybook fully functional in this project by analyzing the codebase,
configuring the preview with the right decorators, and writing example stories for 9 components.

Work through these steps in order. After each story file, run Vitest to verify it renders.
If the test fails, read the error, fix the issue, and re-run until it passes before moving on.

### Step 1: Analyze the codebase

Before writing any stories, understand what the components need to render:

- Scan the project for context providers, theme systems, routers, stores, and i18n setups.
  Look at the app's entry point (e.g. `App.tsx`, `main.tsx`, `layout.tsx`) to see what
  providers wrap the component tree.
- Identify global CSS or style imports required for components to look correct.
- Note any path aliases configured in tsconfig or bundler config.
- Read `.storybook/main.ts` (or `main.js`) to find the `stories` glob patterns.
  Your story files must match those patterns to be picked up by Storybook.

### Step 2: Configure `.storybook/preview.ts` with decorators

Add decorators that wrap every story with the providers your components need.
Without this, most non-trivial components will crash.

If the project uses CSF Factory (look for `definePreview` in `.storybook/preview.ts`):
```ts
// .storybook/preview.ts
import '../src/index.css'; // import global styles

import { definePreview } from 'storybook/preview';

export const config = definePreview({
  decorators: [
    (Story) => (
      <ThemeProvider theme={theme}>
        <MemoryRouter>
          <Story />
        </MemoryRouter>
      </ThemeProvider>
    ),
  ],
});
```

Otherwise:
```ts
// .storybook/preview.ts
import '../src/index.css'; // import global styles

const preview = {
  decorators: [
    (Story) => (
      <ThemeProvider theme={theme}>
        <MemoryRouter>
          <Story />
        </MemoryRouter>
      </ThemeProvider>
    ),
  ],
};
export default preview;
```

Common decorators to add:
- **Theme providers** (e.g. ThemeProvider, MUI ThemeProvider, styled-components, Tailwind)
- **Router** (e.g. MemoryRouter, BrowserRouter mock)
- **State stores** (e.g. Redux Provider, Zustand, Jotai)
- **i18n** (e.g. IntlProvider, I18nextProvider)
- **Global CSS** — import global stylesheets at the top of preview.ts

### Step 3: Write stories for 9 components

Pick 9 real components from the codebase, 3 of each complexity level.
Use the title prefix `AI Generated/<Complexity>/<ComponentName>` so they are grouped
together in the Storybook sidebar.

**Simple (3 components)** — Presentational with few props, no internal state.
Examples: Button, Badge, Avatar, Icon, Label, Chip.
Title format: `AI Generated/Simple/<ComponentName>`

**Medium (3 components)** — Multiple visual variants or composed from simpler components.
Examples: Card, Alert, Input, Select, Tooltip, Tabs.
Title format: `AI Generated/Medium/<ComponentName>`

**Complex (3 components)** — Internal state, side effects, or deep composition.
Examples: Modal, DataTable, Form, Dropdown, Accordion, Sidebar.
Title format: `AI Generated/Complex/<ComponentName>`

For each component, create a `<ComponentName>.stories.ts` file next to the component.
Each file must have at least 2 story exports covering the component's main states.
Make sure the file location and naming matches the `stories` patterns in `.storybook/main.ts`.

If the project uses CSF Factory (look for `definePreview` / `config.meta` patterns):

Story format (CSF Factory — this project uses CSF factories):
```ts
import { config } from '#.storybook/preview';
import { Button } from './Button';

const meta = config.meta({
  title: 'AI Generated/Simple/Button',
  component: Button,
});

export const Default = meta.story({
  args: {
    label: 'Click me',
  },
});

export const Disabled = meta.story({
  args: {
    label: 'Disabled',
    disabled: true,
  },
});
```

Otherwise:

Story format (CSF):
```ts
import type { Meta, StoryObj } from '@storybook/react';
import { Button } from './Button';

const meta = {
  title: 'AI Generated/Simple/Button',
  component: Button,
} satisfies Meta<typeof Button>;

export default meta;
type Story = StoryObj<typeof meta>;

export const Default: Story = {
  args: {
    label: 'Click me',
  },
};

export const Disabled: Story = {
  args: {
    label: 'Disabled',
    disabled: true,
  },
};
```

Rules:
- Every named export is a story. Use `args` to set props.
- Provide all required props via `args` — check the component's types.
- If a component needs per-story decorators (beyond the global ones), add them in the meta.
- Do NOT use `any` types. Use the component's prop types for type safety.

Reference: https://storybook.js.org/docs/latest/writing-stories

### Step 4: Verify each story with Vitest

After writing each story file, immediately verify it:

```bash
npx vitest --project storybook <path-to-story-file>
```

**Self-healing loop — repeat for every story file:**
1. Write/update the story file
2. Run `npx vitest --project storybook <path-to-story-file>`
3. If it fails: read the error output carefully
   - Missing provider → add a decorator in `.storybook/preview.ts` or in the story meta
   - Missing prop → add the required prop to `args`
   - Import error → fix the import path
   - CSS/asset error → add static dirs or import the stylesheet
4. Fix the issue and go back to step 2
5. Once the test passes, move to the next component

After all 9 story files pass individually, run the full suite:
```bash
npx vitest --project storybook
```

### Checklist

- [ ] Analyzed codebase for providers, global styles, and path aliases
- [ ] Read story patterns from `.storybook/main.ts`
- [ ] Configured `.storybook/preview.ts` with necessary decorators
- [ ] Simple component 1: story written and passing
- [ ] Simple component 2: story written and passing
- [ ] Simple component 3: story written and passing
- [ ] Medium component 1: story written and passing
- [ ] Medium component 2: story written and passing
- [ ] Medium component 3: story written and passing
- [ ] Complex component 1: story written and passing
- [ ] Complex component 2: story written and passing
- [ ] Complex component 3: story written and passing
- [ ] Full Vitest suite passes: `npx vitest --project storybook`
- [ ] Run `npx storybook doctor` to check for common issues (version mismatches, duplicated deps, etc.)

transcript.prompt:
Attention: The following instructions must be followed in order to successfully set up Storybook in this project. Do not skip steps or attempt to do them out of order.

Your goal is to make Storybook fully functional in this project by analyzing the codebase,
configuring the preview with the right decorators, and writing example stories for 9 components.

Work through these steps in order. After each story file, run Vitest to verify it renders.
If the test fails, read the error, fix the issue, and re-run until it passes before moving on.

### Step 1: Analyze the codebase

Before writing any stories, understand what the components need to render:

- Scan the project for context providers, theme systems, routers, stores, and i18n setups.
  Look at the app's entry point (e.g. `App.tsx`, `main.tsx`, `layout.tsx`) to see what
  providers wrap the component tree.
- Identify global CSS or style imports required for components to look correct.
- Note any path aliases configured in tsconfig or bundler config.
- Read `.storybook/main.ts` (or `main.js`) to find the `stories` glob patterns.
  Your story files must match those patterns to be picked up by Storybook.

### Step 2: Configure `.storybook/preview.ts` with decorators

Add decorators that wrap every story with the providers your components need.
Without this, most non-trivial components will crash.

If the project uses CSF Factory (look for `definePreview` in `.storybook/preview.ts`):
```ts
// .storybook/preview.ts
import '../src/index.css'; // import global styles

import { definePreview } from 'storybook/preview';

export const config = definePreview({
  decorators: [
    (Story) => (
      <ThemeProvider theme={theme}>
        <MemoryRouter>
          <Story />
        </MemoryRouter>
      </ThemeProvider>
    ),
  ],
});
```

Otherwise:
```ts
// .storybook/preview.ts
import '../src/index.css'; // import global styles

const preview = {
  decorators: [
    (Story) => (
      <ThemeProvider theme={theme}>
        <MemoryRouter>
          <Story />
        </MemoryRouter>
      </ThemeProvider>
    ),
  ],
};
export default preview;
```

Common decorators to add:
- **Theme providers** (e.g. ThemeProvider, MUI ThemeProvider, styled-components, Tailwind)
- **Router** (e.g. MemoryRouter, BrowserRouter mock)
- **State stores** (e.g. Redux Provider, Zustand, Jotai)
- **i18n** (e.g. IntlProvider, I18nextProvider)
- **Global CSS** — import global stylesheets at the top of preview.ts

### Step 3: Write stories for 9 components

Pick 9 real components from the codebase, 3 of each complexity level.
Use the title prefix `AI Generated/<Complexity>/<ComponentName>` so they are grouped
together in the Storybook sidebar.

**Simple (3 components)** — Presentational with few props, no internal state.
Examples: Button, Badge, Avatar, Icon, Label, Chip.
Title format: `AI Generated/Simple/<ComponentName>`

**Medium (3 components)** — Multiple visual variants or composed from simpler components.
Examples: Card, Alert, Input, Select, Tooltip, Tabs.
Title format: `AI Generated/Medium/<ComponentName>`

**Complex (3 components)** — Internal state, side effects, or deep composition.
Examples: Modal, DataTable, Form, Dropdown, Accordion, Sidebar.
Title format: `AI Generated/Complex/<ComponentName>`

For each component, create a `<ComponentName>.stories.ts` file next to the component.
Each file must have at least 2 story exports covering the component's main states.
Make sure the file location and naming matches the `stories` patterns in `.storybook/main.ts`.

If the project uses CSF Factory (look for `definePreview` / `config.meta` patterns):

Story format (CSF Factory — this project uses CSF factories):
```ts
import { config } from '#.storybook/preview';
import { Button } from './Button';

const meta = config.meta({
  title: 'AI Generated/Simple/Button',
  component: Button,
});

export const Default = meta.story({
  args: {
    label: 'Click me',
  },
});

export const Disabled = meta.story({
  args: {
    label: 'Disabled',
    disabled: true,
  },
});
```

Otherwise:

Story format (CSF):
```ts
import type { Meta, StoryObj } from '@storybook/react';
import { Button } from './Button';

const meta = {
  title: 'AI Generated/Simple/Button',
  component: Button,
} satisfies Meta<typeof Button>;

export default meta;
type Story = StoryObj<typeof meta>;

export const Default: Story = {
  args: {
    label: 'Click me',
  },
};

export const Disabled: Story = {
  args: {
    label: 'Disabled',
    disabled: true,
  },
};
```

Rules:
- Every named export is a story. Use `args` to set props.
- Provide all required props via `args` — check the component's types.
- If a component needs per-story decorators (beyond the global ones), add them in the meta.
- Do NOT use `any` types. Use the component's prop types for type safety.

Reference: https://storybook.js.org/docs/latest/writing-stories

### Step 4: Verify each story with Vitest

After writing each story file, immediately verify it:

```bash
npx vitest --project storybook <path-to-story-file>
```

**Self-healing loop — repeat for every story file:**
1. Write/update the story file
2. Run `npx vitest --project storybook <path-to-story-file>`
3. If it fails: read the error output carefully
   - Missing provider → add a decorator in `.storybook/preview.ts` or in the story meta
   - Missing prop → add the required prop to `args`
   - Import error → fix the import path
   - CSS/asset error → add static dirs or import the stylesheet
4. Fix the issue and go back to step 2
5. Once the test passes, move to the next component

After all 9 story files pass individually, run the full suite:
```bash
npx vitest --project storybook
```

### Checklist

- [ ] Analyzed codebase for providers, global styles, and path aliases
- [ ] Read story patterns from `.storybook/main.ts`
- [ ] Configured `.storybook/preview.ts` with necessary decorators
- [ ] Simple component 1: story written and passing
- [ ] Simple component 2: story written and passing
- [ ] Simple component 3: story written and passing
- [ ] Medium component 1: story written and passing
- [ ] Medium component 2: story written and passing
- [ ] Medium component 3: story written and passing
- [ ] Complex component 1: story written and passing
- [ ] Complex component 2: story written and passing
- [ ] Complex component 3: story written and passing
- [ ] Full Vitest suite passes: `npx vitest --project storybook`
- [ ] Run `npx storybook doctor` to check for common issues (version mismatches, duplicated deps, etc.)

        date: 'Mar 16, 2023',
        datetime: "2023-03-16",
        imageUrl: "https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1600096194534-95cf5ece04cf?ixlib=rb-1.2.1&ixid=MnwxMjA3fDB8MHxwaG90by1wYWdlfHx8fGVufDB8fHx8&auto=format&fit=crop&w=776&q=80",

        blogPost: `In today's digital age, social media has become an integral part of our lives, and Instagram, with its visually captivating platform, stands out as a popular choice for content creators looking to start a blog. Whether you're passionate about fashion, travel, food, fitness, or any other niche, Instagram offers an excellent opportunity to showcase your creativity and engage with a global audience. So, if you're eager to share your passions with the world, here's a step-by-step guide on how to start an Instagram blog and make it a resounding success.\n\n1. Define Your Niche:\nBefore diving headfirst into Instagram blogging, it's crucial to identify your niche. Determine what interests you the most and where your expertise lies. A well-defined niche helps you stand out in a sea of content creators and attracts a dedicated audience genuinely interested in your content.\n\n2. Create a Compelling Instagram Handle:\nYour Instagram handle is essentially your brand name, so make it catchy and reflective of your niche. Keep it simple, memorable, and easy to spell to ensure that potential followers can find and remember you easily.\n\n3. Develop a Cohesive Aesthetic:\nInstagram is a visual platform, and maintaining a consistent and attractive aesthetic is key to grabbing attention and retaining followers. Choose a color palette, editing style, and overall theme that aligns with your niche and reflects your personality.\n\n4. Craft an Engaging Bio:\nYour Instagram bio is your chance to make a great first impression. Use it to concisely describe who you are, what your blog is about, and what value you provide to your followers. Include relevant keywords and a clear call-to-action (CTA) to encourage visitors to follow you.\n\n5. Share High-Quality Content:\nQuality is paramount on Instagram. Invest in a good camera or use your smartphone's advanced camera features to capture stunning images. Ensure your photos are well-lit, well-composed, and visually appealing. Additionally, use engaging captions that provide context, ask questions, or encourage interaction.\n\n6. Plan Your Content Strategy:\nConsistency is key to Instagram success. Plan your content in advance using content calendars or scheduling tools. This will help you maintain a steady flow of posts and ensure that you cover a diverse range of topics within your niche.\n\n7. Utilize Instagram Stories and IGTV:\nInstagram Stories and IGTV are powerful tools for connecting with your audience in a more authentic and engaging way. Use Stories to share behind-the-scenes content, polls, Q&A sessions, and announcements. IGTV allows for longer-form content, giving you an opportunity to showcase in-depth tutorials, travel vlogs, or interviews.\n\n8. Engage with Your Audience:\nInteract with your followers regularly by responding to comments, answering direct messages, and acknowledging user-generated content (UGC). Building a strong relationship with your audience fosters loyalty and encourages them to support your blog in the long run.\n\n9. Collaborate and Network:\nCollaborations with other content creators, brands, or influencers can significantly boost your exposure. Look for like-minded individuals or companies within your niche and explore partnership opportunities that align with your brand values.\n\n10. Monitor Analytics and Adapt:\nKeep a close eye on Instagram Insights to analyze the performance of your posts, identify the best times to post, and understand your audience demographics. Use this data to adapt your content strategy and optimize your Instagram blog for better engagement and growth.\n\nStarting an Instagram blog may seem overwhelming, but with passion, dedication, and a well-thought-out approach, success is within reach. Remember, it's not just about followers and likes; it's about creating meaningful connections and sharing your unique voice with the world. So, take the leap, stay true to yourself, and watch your Instagram blog thrive!`

    },

        imageUrl:

        blogPost: `
Instagram is a powerful platform for engaging with your audience, but knowing what to post can sometimes be challenging. To help you keep your feed interesting and your followers engaged, we've put together a list of content ideas that you can use to make your Instagram shine!\n\n
1. Behind-the-Scenes:\n
Take your followers behind the scenes and show them the process of creating your products or services. People love to see the human side of your brand and get a glimpse of what goes on behind closed doors.\n\n
2. User-Generated Content (UGC):\n
Encourage your followers to create content featuring your products or services and share it on their own profiles. UGC not only gives your brand credibility but also helps foster a sense of community among your followers.\n\n
3. Inspirational Quotes:\n
Share motivational and inspirational quotes that align with your brand values. Quotes are highly shareable and can resonate with your audience on a personal level.\n\n
4. Product or Service Highlights:\n
Highlight your best-selling products or most popular services. Showcase their features and benefits to entice your audience to make a purchase.\n\n
5. Tutorials and How-Tos:\n
Educate your followers with step-by-step tutorials and how-to guides related to your industry. Providing value through educational content can establish your brand as an authority in your niche.\n\n
6. Customer Testimonials:\n
Share positive feedback and testimonials from satisfied customers. Testimonials build trust and social proof, which can encourage others to do business with you.\n\n
7. Polls and Surveys:\n
Engage your audience by running polls and surveys in your Instagram Stories. This not only encourages interaction but also helps you gather valuable insights from your followers.\n\n
8. Throwback Posts:\n
Take a trip down memory lane and share old photos or milestones from your brand's history. Nostalgic content can evoke positive emotions and create a sense of nostalgia among your audience.\n\n
9. Seasonal Content:\n
Tailor your content to fit holidays, seasons, or special occasions. Seasonal posts show that your brand is current and relevant.\n\n
10. Fun Facts and Trivia:\n
Share interesting and fun facts related to your industry or niche. Fun and quirky content can entertain your audience and keep them coming back for more.\n\n
11. Collaborations and Partnerships:\n
Highlight any collaborations or partnerships you have with other brands or influencers. Collaborative content can introduce your brand to a new audience and increase your reach.\n\n
12. Company Culture:\n
Give your followers a glimpse into your company culture and values. Share team photos, company events, and charitable initiatives.\n\n
Remember, the key to a successful Instagram feed is to mix up your content and keep it engaging. Experiment with different types of posts and see what resonates best with your audience. Happy posting!`

Instagram Creator Studio offers a range of features to help you manage and analyze your content. You can schedule and publish posts, view post insights, manage comments and direct messages, and track audience demographics.\n\n

One of the key benefits of Instagram Creator Studio is the ability to schedule your posts in advance. This feature allows you to plan and organize your content calendar, ensuring a consistent posting schedule.\n\n
